segments.s049-g25: This is a stacked noun phrase in apposition: 'سران سپه' (the leaders/heads of the army) followed by another title 'مهتران دلیر' (the brave chiefs). Word order is noun + modifier(s), and the plural nouns mark a group of commanding figures. 'مهتران' is an older/formal word for senior men or chiefs; 'دلیر' is an adjective meaning 'brave'.
segments.s049-g26: گذشتهٔ جمعِ فعلی: 'کشیدند' is a past plural verb here meaning 'drew up' (i.e., formed/drew a line). Its object is 'صف' (battle line), followed by a prepositional/location phrase 'پیش سالار شیر' (in front of / before the commander, the Lion). The order Verb + object + prepositional phrase is typical of Persian narrative poetry.
segments.s049-g27: 'به سالار گفتند' = 'they said to the commander' (preposition + indirect object followed by past plural verb 'گفتند'). The reported clause 'ما بنده‌ایم' uses present tense/copula '-ایم' to state 'we are (your) servants' — a conventional humble formula in courtly speech.
segments.s049-g28: This clause gives a reason/loyalty statement: 'خود' is emphatic ('we ourselves'); 'اندر جهان' = 'in the world' (poetic preposition + noun); 'شاه را' uses the object/beneficiary marker 'را' after 'king' to indicate for whom they live; 'زنده‌ایم' is present 'we are alive' = 'we live (for the king)'. The sequence emphasizes devotion to the king.
segments.s049-g29: 'چو' here introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if'; 'فرمان دهد' uses the present/subjunctive form 'دهد' (classical usage to express a future/conditional event) — 'when he gives the order'. The main clause begins with 'ما' and the verb 'کنیم' (subjunctive/future 'we will do'). The word 'همیدون' is uncertain/obscure here and functions as the object or complement of 'کنیم' (likely a place or term for the battlefield); its exact sense is unclear in the gloss provided.
segments.s049-g30: Poetic causative/result clause: 'زمین را' (earth/ground with the object marker 'را') is what they will affect; 'ز خون' uses the poetic preposition 'ز' = 'with/from' to indicate material ('with blood'); 'رود جیحون' is a compound noun 'the Jeyhun River' (رود + proper name); final verb 'کنیم' is the same subjunctive/optative 'we will cause/make'. The whole means they will cause the ground to flow with blood as far as the Jeyhun.
segments.s049-g31: 'سوی' = 'toward' + goal 'خیمه خویش' ('their own tent') where 'خیمه' is 'tent' and the possessive is formed by 'خوی' + 'ش' (poetic splitting of 'خویش'); 'باز آمدند' is past plural 'they returned/returned again'. Word order places the direction phrase before the verb, as common in Persian narrative.
segments.s049-g32: 'همه' (all/everyone) is the subject; 'با سری کینه ساز' is an adverbial phrase 'with a head/heart that makes hatred' — here 'سری' functions as 'head/mind' and 'کینه‌ساز' is a compound where 'ساز' acts like 'maker/producer' (i.e., 'hatred-making' = 'full of grudge'); 'آمدند' closes with past plural 'they came'. The phrase idiomatically means they returned filled with hatred/vengeful intent.
segments.s049-g9: میان (object) + بسته (adjective 'closed') + دارید (2nd‑person plural present of داشتن used here in the sense 'keep, hold'): literally 'you keep the middle closed.' و links this clause to the next. بیدار (adjective 'awake/alert') + بید (archaic present/subjunctive/optative copula 'be' used as an exhortation): 'be watchful.' Word order places the adjective before the copula for a terse, poetic command.
segments.s049-g10: همه (subject 'all/everyone') + در پناه (prepositional phrase 'in the refuge/protection') frames a clause about everyone's state. جهاندار بید splits off as a short clause: جهاندار (the world‑ruler/sovereign) + بید (here 'is awake' — bید as an archaic predicative meaning 'awake'): 'the sovereign is awake.' The sentence thus gives a protective assurance after the command.
segments.s049-g11: کسی (indefinite 'someone') + کو (archaic relative particle here = 'who'; an older form introducing the relative clause) + شود (3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of شدن, 'becomes/is') + کشته (past participle 'killed/slain') gives 'someone who is slain.' زین رزمگاه is an archaic fused prepositional phrase (ز + این = 'from/at this') + رزمگاه ('battlefield'), so the whole clause reads 'someone who is slain in/at this battlefield.'
segments.s049-g12: بهشتی (noun/adjective 'a dweller of paradise' or 'heavenly') + بود (past/copular 'was' used here to assert the state or consequence) yields 'will be (a) paradiseresident.' شسته (past participle 'washed') + پاک (adjective 'clean/pure') + از (preposition 'of/from') + گناه ('sin') forms the adjectival phrase 'washed clean of sin.' Together: 'will be a paradise‑dweller, washed clean of sin.'
segments.s049-g13: هر آن کس که is a fixed classical formula meaning 'whichever person who / anyone who' (هر 'each/every' + آن 'that/the' + کس 'person' + که relative 'who'). از (preposition 'from') + لشکر (army) + چین و روم (place names 'China and Rome/Byzantium') specifies the source/agents: 'from the armies of China and Rome.'
segments.s049-g14: بریزند (3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive or jussive of ریختن, here general/habitual 'they spill/pour') + خون ('blood' or 'bloodshed') states the violent action. و links to بگیرند (3rd‑person plural subjunctive of گرفتن, 'they take/seize') + بوم (noun 'homeland/native soil'), i.e. 'they spill blood and seize the land.' The verb forms are plural and jussive/generalizing, describing the actions of those armies.
segments.s049-g15: همه (subject 'all/everyone') + نیکنامند (predicate, 3rd‑person plural 'are of good name / are well‑known') gives 'all are honorable.' تا جاودان uses تا with a temporal extent 'until/through' and جاودان 'eternal' to mean 'forever' or 'to eternity.' Together: 'all are well‑renowned to eternity.'
segments.s049-g16: بمانند is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive of ماندن 'remain/last' ('they remain' or 'may they remain'). با ('with') + فره ('farr', the divine royal glory) + موبدان (plural of موبد, 'mōbeds' — Zoroastrian priests) forms an instrumental/associative phrase: 'they endure with the divine glory of the priests.' The construction links the people's lasting fame to priestly divine radiance.
segments.s049-g1: بدان گه که = به آن گاه که, a temporal phrase 'at that time / when' (poetic contraction). روشن جهان is adjective + noun ('bright world') with the adjective preceding the noun for emphasis; تیره گشت is a past, intransitive verb phrase 'became dark' (گشت = 'became/turned'). The clause is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by که linking to what follows.
segments.s049-g2: طلایه پراگنده is a noun + adjective phrase 'the vanguard (طلایه) scattered/widespread (پراگنده)'; in poetry adjectives often appear after the noun. بر گرد دشت is a locative expression 'around the plain' where بر + گرد together give the sense 'around/about' and دشت is the object/place.
segments.s049-g3: به پیش سپه is a prepositional/location phrase 'in front of / before the host' (به + پیش + سپه). قارن رزم زن is an appositive/name + epithet: a name (قارن) followed by a compound epithet where رزم (battle) + زن (literally 'strike' used to form an agent/epithet 'one who strikes in battle' = 'warrior').
segments.s049-g4: This short string lists further epithets/titles without overt connectors: ابا رای زن is another compound epithet (ابا + رای + زن) where زن again functions as an agentive element '‑striker' or '‑wielder' attached to preceding element(s); سرو شاه یمن is a kenning/title 'the cypress (syrv) of the king of Yemen' (سرو = tall/notable person as a metaphorical epithet). The whole segment is a compact list of names/epithets identifying leaders in the vanguard.
segments.s049-g5: خروشی برآمد ز پیش سپاه is a straightforward clause: خروشی (a roar/cry) is the subject, برآمد is past 'arose/issued forth', ز (poetic form of از) means 'from', and پیش سپاه 'the front of the host' is the source/location. Word order is subject + verb + source phrase.
segments.s049-g6: که‌ای here is an archaic/exclamatory vocative (combining که + ای) introducing the direct address 'O ...!'; نامداران و مردان شاه is a vocative list 'renowned ones and the king's men/warriors'. The particle introduces the call directed to those persons.
segments.s049-g7: بکوشید is a past/plural or narrative-form of کوشیدن 'to strive/attempt' used here as 'they strove / they cried (out) / they asserted'. کاین is the fused ک + این = 'that this', جنگ is 'war', and آهرمنست is an older form meaning 'is Ahriman / is demonic' (آهرمن = Ahriman/evil spirit) — the clause reports what was being declared about the war.
segments.s049-g8: همان درد و کین است is a nominal comment 'it is the same pain/suffering and hatred/vengeance' (همان = 'the same; that very'), followed by و خون خستنست which strings 'and blood + خستنست'; the final verb form is archaic/poetic. The clause sums up the nature of the war as suffering, enmity, and bloody exhaustion — note the compact, poetic stacking of nouns and an older verbal ending.
segments.s049-g17: هم begins the clause as an adverb meaning “also/too.” از شاه is a prepositional phrase (“from the king”) that precedes the verb in this poetic word order. یابند is a 3rd person plural verb meaning “obtain/attain” (classical/subjunctive form is used narratively) and takes the coordinated direct objects دیهیم و تخت (“lands/village‑hold(s) and throne”) that follow it.
segments.s049-g18: This string gives two source phrases introduced by the poetic preposition ز (“from”). سالار زر is a genitival phrase “the lord/master of gold” (i.e. wealth), and ز دادار بخت names a divine source “from the Creator (دادار) — (comes) fate/fortune (بخت).” The verb from the previous segment is understood to govern these ‘from’ phrases.
segments.s049-g19: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” پیدا شود is a light‑verb construction where پیدا (visible/apparent) + شود (becomes) = “appears / becomes visible.” پاک modifies روز سپید (“the pure, bright day”); poetic word order places the clause predicate before its noun.
segments.s049-g20: دو بهره is a quantity phrase “two portions/turns.” بپیماید is a verb meaning “to traverse/complete” (here: to make/complete two turns). از چرخ شید is a genitival/ablative phrase “from the wheel of the beloved” (چرخ = wheel/fate; شید = the beloved), the source or locus of those two portions.
segments.s049-g21: ببندید is an imperative/optative (2nd‑person plural) meaning “bind/tie/close” — here instructing to form or hold ranks. یکسر is an adverb “entirely/at once,” and میان یلی is a prepositional phrase “among the valiant/warriors,” giving the location of the action.
segments.s049-g22: This phrase lists the arms with which they should be formed: گرز and خنجر کابلی are coordinated by و and both are governed by the instrumental preposition با (here echoed as ابا for metrical/poetic effect). In plain meaning: “with a mace and with a Kabul dagger.”
segments.s049-g23: بدارید is an imperative/command form from داشتن “to hold/keep” (2nd‑person plural) meaning “hold/keep.” یکسر modifies it as “entirely/without break.” همه جای خویش is the object phrase “all your places/positions” — خوی + ش together form the reflexive/possessive ‘خویش’ (“one’s own”), expressed here as separate elements for metrical reasons.
segments.s049-g24: یکی (one) with از دگر (from the other) yields the idiom “one after another.” پای is the noun “foot,” منهید here functions as an archaic/poetic imperative meaning “set / put” (i.e. step); the precise lemma sense listed does not match this poetic verbal use. پیش is an adverb “forward / ahead,” completing the command “step forward one by one.”

sentences.s049-s13: The army's leaders, the brave chiefs, drew up a battle line before the commander, the Lion.
sentences.s049-s14: They said to the commander, 'We are your servants; we ourselves live in this world for the king.'
sentences.s049-s15: When he gives the command, we will take the field; we will make the earth run with blood up to the Jeyhun.
sentences.s049-s16: They returned to their tents; all came back full of hatred.
sentences.s049-s5: Keep the centre closed and be watchful; all are under the protection — the sovereign is awake.
sentences.s049-s6: Whoever is slain on this battlefield will be a denizen of paradise, washed clean of sin.
sentences.s049-s7: Anyone who, from the armies of China and Rome, spill blood and seize the land —
sentences.s049-s8: All will be well‑renowned and endure for ever, remaining with the divine glory of the mōbeds.
sentences.s049-s1: At that time, when the bright world darkened, the vanguard scattered around the plain.
sentences.s049-s2: Before the host stood Qaren the battle-striker — Aba-rây-zan, the cypress (tall one) of the king of Yemen.
sentences.s049-s3: A cry arose from the front of the host: 'O famed men and warriors of the king!'
sentences.s049-s4: They cried that this war is of Ahriman; it is the same suffering and hatred, and blood wears (people) out.
sentences.s049-s9: They too obtain from the king lands and the throne; (they obtain) gold from the lord and fate from the Creator.
sentences.s049-s10: When the pure, bright day appears, it completes two turns of the beloved’s wheel.
sentences.s049-s11: Form a single, continuous line among the valiant — with maces and with Kabul daggers.
sentences.s049-s12: Hold firmly all your places; one after another set your foot forward.
